raw_case:
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis mimicking multisystem pyoderma gangrenosum: A case report
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis, a type of small-medium vessel vasculitis, can pose diagnostic challenges due to its diverse clinical manifestations. We present a case that mimicked multisystem pyoderma gangrenosum with cutaneous ulceration, renal sparing, and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody negativity. Although antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody is positive in approximately 90% of cases of granulomatosis with polyangiitis, its negativity should not exclude the diagnosis. Additionally, pyoderma gangrenosum-like cutaneous ulcers should prompt consideration of granulomatosis with polyangiitis, especially in the context of multisystem disease presentation. This case underscores the importance of maintaining a high suspicion for granulomatosis with polyangiitis in patients presenting with cutaneous ulceration and respiratory tract disease.

Introduction

Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), formerly known as Wegener’s granulomatosis, is the most common type of antineutrophilic cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis, affecting 10–20 individuals per one million worldwide annually.
1
 The peak incidence of GPA occurs between the ages of 64 and 75. Characterized by a wide range of presentations that can involve various organ systems (Table 1),
2
 GPA is associated with significant morbidity and mortality.
1
 The life expectancy for individuals with untreated GPA is, on average, 5 months; however, remission can be achieved in more than 90% of patients with appropriate treatment.
2
 Treatment of GPA involves immunosuppressive agents such as corticosteroids, cyclophosphamide, rituximab, methotrexate, azathioprine, and plasmapheresis.
1
 Given its significant morbidity and mortality, timely diagnosis and management is imperative. We present a case of suspected ANCA-negative GPA that mimicked multisystem pyoderma gangrenosum (PG). This case highlights features of GPA to assist in future diagnoses, particularly when associated with ANCA-negativity or PG-like cutaneous lesions.

Case report

A 58-year-old male with a past medical history of intranasal cocaine use, chronic sinusitis, and epistaxis was being investigated by respirology for cavitary lung lesions with radiologic progression despite antibiotics. Autoimmune workup was negative, including ANCA. Lung core biopsy #1 was taken (Table 2), leading to a potential diagnosis of organizing pneumonia. Prednisone 0.625 mg/kg daily was started, resulting in a significant decrease in the size of the lung lesions on computed tomography (CT).One month later, during a prednisone taper, the patient developed painful bilateral lower extremity cutaneous ulcerations. Physical exam revealed multiple well-demarcated ulcers with raised, violaceous borders. Repeat autoimmune workup was negative. Dermatology was consulted and diagnosed cutaneous PG. A skin biopsy was not performed at this time due to concern of pathergy. The ulcers responded well to prednisone 0.75 mg/kg daily and topical clobetasol ointment 0.05% with dressing changes.A connection between the cutaneous PG-like lesions and lung nodules was considered. The patient did not have any signs or symptoms of inflammatory bowel disease, arthritis, or other autoimmune connective tissue diseases. The possibility of malignancy was also considered. Before further workup could be performed, his ulcers worsened during a prednisone taper, and he was admitted to the hospital for pain control.While in the hospital, he was seen by dermatology, otolaryngology, respirology, hematology, rheumatology, and infectious diseases. A cutaneous ulcer biopsy showed features consistent with PG with no evidence of vasculitis (Table 2). Serum protein electrophoresis and eosinophil levels were normal. Head CT showed opacification of the right maxillary sinus with loss of the medial sinus wall and a large midline septal defect. Nasal mucosa biopsy #1 and lung core biopsy #2 were performed (Table 2). Given the lack of diagnostic clarity, lung wedge resections were taken (Table 2). The findings were signed as consistent with PG given the clinical suspicion of the same in his skin. The patient was diagnosed with multisystem PG and was started on cyclosporine 5 mg/kg daily, which resulted in a temporary improvement of his cutaneous ulcers (Figure 1). Infliximab 5 mg/kg every 6 weeks was subsequently initiated which resulted in dramatic ulcer healing (Figure 2).In the year following discharge from the hospital, his ulcers worsened, and he developed sinus pain, progressive proptosis, and painful periocular swelling. Nasal mucosa biopsy #2 and palate biopsy #1 were taken (Table 2). Repeat ANCA was negative. Head CT showed right-sided pansinusitis and new destruction of the orbital floor and hard palate. Periocular discharge was drained and cultured, showing an abundance of neutrophils and no infection. Prednisone 1 mg/kg daily and infliximab 10 mg/kg every 4 weeks resulted in rapid improvements in his facial pain, proptosis, and periocular swelling. Given the bony necrosis and respiratory tract involvement, the patient was diagnosed with ANCA-negative GPA with PG-like cutaneous changes. The patient subsequently expired unexpectedly at home. An autopsy was declined by the family.

Discussion

We present a case of ANCA-negative GPA with renal sparing and PG-like cutaneous ulcers that mimicked multisystem PG. In GPA, up to 90% of cases are positive for ANCA.
2
 Given this, it is imperative that physicians consider GPA in patients with multisystem inflammation or vasculitis, even in the context of a negative ANCA. Diagnosing ANCA-negative GPA can be further complicated when classic findings such as renal involvement are absent.Renal sparing is often seen in patients with GPA, particularly early in the disease course or when ANCA is negative.1,3 The classic triad seen in GPA includes involvement of the upper respiratory tract, lower respiratory tract, and kidneys.
1
 Although 80% of patients have renal involvement within 2 years of disease onset, only 10%–20% have renal involvement at the time of presentation.
1
 Additionally, ANCA is negative in an estimated 40% of active, renal-sparing diseases.
3
 Similarly to renal involvement, other features of GPA are often absent on workup.Histological findings of GPA can be absent. The histological findings in GPA include vasculitis, granulomas, and giant cells.
4
 In GPA, vasculitis and granulomatous inflammation may only be seen in 21% of head and neck biopsies.
5
 Further, only 47% of nasal biopsies in GPA reveal scattered giant cells. In the case presented, multiple biopsies were nonspecific and lacked features classic for GPA. GPA exhibits variable histological findings, and its clinical presentation, including cutaneous manifestations, can also vary significantly.GPA can present with cutaneous changes, including PG-like ulcers. Although up to 50% of cases of GPA are found to have cutaneous involvement,
1
 PG-like ulcers are a rare manifestation.6,7 PG is a rare neutrophilic inflammatory condition that classically presents as painful cutaneous pustules or nodules that evolve into ulcers with purulent drainage and violaceous, undermined borders.
8
 Extracutaneous manifestations of PG can also be seen, with pulmonary involvement being the most common.
9
 Similarly to GPA, there is a lack of gold standard diagnostic tests for PG.
9
 Findings of PG on histopathology are often nonspecific, with neutrophilic inflammation being the most common. It can thus be difficult to differentiate between multisystem PG and GPA with skin and pulmonary involvement.In summary, diagnosing GPA can pose significant challenges, particularly when ANCA is negative. It is crucial for physicians to consider GPA when ANCA is negative, even if other typical features such as kidney involvement and histologic findings are absent. Additionally, GPA should be considered in patients presenting with PG-like ulcers, particularly when accompanied by inflammation or vasculitis affecting other systems.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 58-year-old male.
- Chief Complaint: None explicitly noted; major concerns include cutaneous ulcerations, respiratory involvement, and sinus and orbital issues.
- History of Present Illness: Chronic sinusitis, epistaxis, cavitary lung lesions unresponsive to antibiotics, and subsequent development of painful bilateral lower extremity cutaneous ulcerations following prednisone taper. Later symptoms included sinus pain, proptosis, and periocular swelling.
- Past Medical History: Intranasal cocaine use, chronic sinusitis, and epistaxis.
- Personal History: Intranasal cocaine use.
- Family History: None mentioned.
- Physical Examination: Well-demarcated ulcers with raised violaceous borders; sinus opacification, midline septal defect, destruction of the orbital floor, and hard palate.
- Ancillary Tests: Negative autoimmune and ANCA workup, normal serum protein electrophoresis and eosinophil levels, abnormal histologic findings (PG on skin biopsy; nonspecific findings lacking classic signs of GPA), radiologic findings of lung and sinus involvement, and culture showing neutrophils without infection.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Multisystem Pyoderma Gangrenosum (PG)**: Initially considered due to the presence of PG-like cutaneous ulcers and negative ANCA. Skin biopsy findings and temporary response to immunosuppressive therapy supported this diagnosis. However, recurrent symptoms, progression of disease (sinus and bone destruction), and multisystem involvement complicated the diagnosis.
2. **Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA)**: Despite ANCA negativity, sinus and lung involvement, bone necrosis, and systemic inflammation raised suspicion for ANCA-negative GPA. Renal sparing and absence of definitive histological findings are known in GPA and aligned with the clinical presentation.
3. **Infectious, Autoimmune, or Malignant Conditions**: Infections were ruled out by culture; autoimmune conditions like inflammatory bowel disease or arthritis were unlikely due to a lack of systemic features; malignancy was ruled out by histopathological assessments.

Through systemic exclusion, ANCA-negative GPA with PG-like ulceration emerged as the robust diagnosis based on multisystem disease and evidence of granulomatous inflammation despite negative lab and biopsy findings.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: ANCA-negative Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA) with Pyoderma Gangrenosum (PG)-like skin presentations. The diagnosis was based on progressive multisystem involvement (especially respiratory and bony destruction), PG-like cutaneous manifestations, and systemic inflammation, with ANCA-negative findings and renal sparing.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: ANCA-negative Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA) with Pyoderma Gangrenosum (PG)-like presentations.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 58-year-old male with a history of chronic sinusitis, epistaxis, and intranasal cocaine use. His main issues now are cutaneous ulcers, respiratory problems, and sinus/orbital involvement. 

First, the chronic sinusitis and epistaxis could be due to his cocaine use. Cocaine can cause nasal septum perforation, which he has. But then he developed cavitary lung lesions that didn't respond to antibiotics. That's a red flag. Infections like TB or fungal could cause cavities, but antibiotics didn't work. Maybe it's not bacterial. Then, after stopping prednisone, he got painful leg ulcers. Steroid taper leading to worsening suggests an inflammatory condition flaring up.

His physical exam shows well-demarcated ulcers with violaceous borders. Skin biopsy showed pyoderma gangrenosum (PG). PG is often associated with autoimmune diseases like IBD or rheumatoid arthritis, but his autoimmune workup was negative. ANCA was negative too, which makes me think maybe not granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), which is usually ANCA-positive. But wait, some GPA cases are ANCA-negative, especially the localized ones.

The sinus and orbital involvement: destruction of the orbital floor and hard palate. That's destructive lesions. GPA can cause that. Midline destruction can also be from cocaine-induced midline destructive lesion, but combined with lung cavities and skin ulcers, maybe not just cocaine. But the biopsy didn't show classic GPA features like granulomas or vasculitis. Hmm. The histology for PG is non-specific, so maybe it's a different process.

He had a negative ANCA, but sometimes in cocaine-induced midline destructive lesions, there's a secondary ANCA-positive status, but here it's negative. So maybe it's not that. The lung cavities and sinus destruction could be from something else. What about infectious causes? Cultures showed neutrophils but no infection. So maybe not. 

Another angle: cocaine use can lead to a condition called cocaine-induced midline destructive lesion (CIMDL), which can mimic GPA. CIMDL can cause nasal septum perforation, sinusitis, and even palate destruction. But typically, CIMDL doesn't involve the lungs. However, if he's using crack cocaine, maybe there's lung involvement. But his lung lesions are cavitary. Cocaine-induced lung issues might present differently, like pneumonitis or hemorrhage. 

Wait, the patient has both upper respiratory (sinus, orbital), lower respiratory (lung cavities), and skin involvement. The classic triad for GPA is upper respiratory, lower respiratory, and kidneys. But he doesn't mention renal issues here. However, not all cases have renal involvement. But ANCA is negative. So maybe it's a variant of GPA that's ANCA-negative. 

Alternatively, could this be a cocaine-induced vasculitis or another autoimmune disease? His workup for autoimmune diseases was negative. The skin ulcers were PG-like, but PG is a diagnosis of exclusion. The lack of response to antibiotics and response to steroids (since prednisone was tapered, maybe he was on it before?) might suggest an inflammatory condition. 

Another thought: maybe it's eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA), but his eosinophils are normal. So less likely. Microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) usually has positive ANCA and more renal involvement. 

Putting it all together: The patient has a history of cocaine use leading to midline destruction, but also systemic issues like lung cavities and skin ulcers. The biopsy didn't confirm GPA, but the clinical picture overlaps. Cocaine use can sometimes induce ANCA-positive vasculitis, but here ANCA is negative. The skin ulcers as PG might be a clue. There's a condition called "PACNS" (primary angiitis of the CNS) but that's not fitting here. 

Wait, there's a condition called "Cocaine-induced granulomatosis with polyangiitis-like syndrome". Cocaine can induce a syndrome that mimics GPA, especially with levamisole-adulterated cocaine. Levamisole can cause vasculitis, skin necrosis, and ANCA positivity (usually p-ANCA). But here ANCA is negative. However, maybe without levamisole, just cocaine. But how to differentiate?

Alternatively, maybe it's a combined effect of cocaine causing local destruction and a separate systemic vasculitis. But the lack of response to antibiotics and the response to steroids (assuming prednisone was helping before the taper) supports an autoimmune/vasculitic process. 

Given the negative ANCA and histology not showing vasculitis or granulomas, but the clinical features resembling GPA, could this be a limited form or early stage? Or perhaps another type of vasculitis. But without the classic histology, it's tricky. 

Another possibility: necrotizing sarcoid granulomatosis, but again, histology isn't showing granulomas. 

The key here is the combination of cocaine use and multi-organ involvement. The differential is between cocaine-induced damage and systemic vasculitis. Since the ANCA is negative and biopsy doesn't confirm GPA, maybe the diagnosis is cocaine-induced midline destructive lesion with secondary complications. But the lung cavities complicate that. Alternatively, could the lung cavities be due to septic emboli or other infections? But cultures were negative. 

Wait, the radiologic findings show lung and sinus involvement. If it's CIMDL, the lungs might not be involved. So maybe there's two processes: CIMDL for the sinus issues and another condition for the lungs and skin. But PG is associated with systemic diseases. Alternatively, could the lung cavities be due to PG? No, PG is skin. 

Alternatively, maybe the lung cavities are from another cause. But the patient's skin and lung issues developed after prednisone taper. That suggests an inflammatory condition that was suppressed by steroids. 

In summary, the most likely diagnosis despite negative ANCA is granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), possibly ANCA-negative variant, given the triad of upper respiratory, lower respiratory, and skin involvement, even though the biopsy wasn't classic. Alternatively, cocaine-induced lesions plus another condition. But given the destructive sinus and orbital lesions plus lung cavities, GPA is the top consideration. The negative ANCA and biopsy make it challenging, but not all cases are textbook. The response to steroids (before taper) also supports an autoimmune process.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient's history of chronic sinusitis, epistaxis, and intranasal cocaine use initially suggests cocaine-induced midline destructive lesions (CIMDL), which can cause septal perforation and sinus damage. However, the presence of cavitary lung lesions unresponsive to antibiotics and cutaneous ulcerations after steroid taper expands the differential beyond local cocaine toxicity.  
<step 2> The combination of upper respiratory (sinus/orbital destruction), lower respiratory (cavitary lung lesions), and cutaneous (ulcers with pyoderma gangrenosum-like features) involvement aligns with granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA). While ANCA is negative in this case, 10-15% of GPA patients are ANCA-negative, particularly in localized or early disease.  
<step 3> Cocaine use, especially when adulterated with levamisole, can mimic vasculitis with skin necrosis and ANCA positivity. However, the absence of ANCA positivity and the systemic involvement (lungs, orbit, palate) argue against pure cocaine/levamisole toxicity.  
<step 4> The lack of classic histologic findings (granulomas, vasculitis) on biopsy complicates the diagnosis, but GPA can exhibit nonspecific inflammation in early stages. The destructive midline lesions and orbital involvement are highly suggestive of GPA, even with atypical histology.  
<step 5> Alternative diagnoses like infectious etologies (e.g., tuberculosis, fungal) are less likely given negative cultures and lack of response to antibiotics. Other vasculitides (e.g., eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, microscopic polyangiitis) are ruled out by normal eosinophil levels, absence of renal involvement, and ANCA negativity.  

### Answer:
Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), ANCA-negative variant.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient's history of chronic sinusitis, epistaxis, and intranasal cocaine use initially suggests cocaine-induced midline destructive lesions (CIMDL), which can cause septal perforation and sinus damage. However, the presence of cavitary lung lesions unresponsive to antibiotics and cutaneous ulcerations after steroid taper expands the differential beyond local cocaine toxicity.

The combination of upper respiratory (sinus/orbital destruction), lower respiratory (cavitary lung lesions), and cutaneous (ulcers with pyoderma gangrenosum-like features) involvement aligns with granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA). While ANCA is negative in this case, 10-15% of GPA patients are ANCA-negative, particularly in localized or early disease.

Cocaine use, especially when adulterated with levamisole, can mimic vasculitis with skin necrosis and ANCA positivity. However, the absence of ANCA positivity and the systemic involvement (lungs, orbit, palate) argue against pure cocaine/levamisole toxicity.

The lack of classic histologic findings (granulomas, vasculitis) on biopsy complicates the diagnosis, but GPA can exhibit nonspecific inflammation in early stages. The destructive midline lesions and orbital involvement are highly suggestive of GPA, even with atypical histology.

Alternative diagnoses like infectious etologies (e.g., tuberculosis, fungal) are less likely given negative cultures and lack of response to antibiotics. Other vasculitides (e.g., eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, microscopic polyangiitis) are ruled out by normal eosinophil levels, absence of renal involvement, and ANCA negativity.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial consideration of Multisystem Pyoderma Gangrenosum (PG) due to PG-like cutaneous ulcers and negative ANCA. Skin biopsy and temporary response to immunosuppressive therapy supported this diagnosis.
Step 2: Recurrent symptoms, progression of disease (sinus and bone destruction), and multisystem involvement complicated the initial PG diagnosis.
Step 3: Suspicion for ANCA-negative Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA) raised due to sinus and lung involvement, bone necrosis, and systemic inflammation, despite ANCA negativity.
Step 4: Renal sparing and absence of definitive histological findings align with the clinical presentation of GPA.

Step 5: Infections ruled out by culture; autoimmune conditions like inflammatory bowel disease or arthritis unlikely due to lack of systemic features; malignancy ruled out by histopathological assessments.
Step 6: Systemic exclusion led to ANCA-negative GPA with PG-like ulceration as the robust diagnosis based on multisystem disease and granulomatous inflammation.
Step 7: Diagnosis confirmed by progressive multisystem involvement (especially respiratory and bony destruction), PG-like cutaneous manifestations, and systemic inflammation.
Step 8: ANCA-negative findings and renal sparing further support the diagnosis of ANCA-negative GPA with PG-like skin presentations.
